summary: Gruber examines Google's new Gboard iOS keyboard, which integrates Google Search directly into the typing experience. His initial privacy concerns are allayed by Google's clear privacy policy stating only search queries are sent to Google, with all other typing data staying on-device. He notes the curious design choice of mimicking iOS's native keyboard appearance rather than using Google's Material Design, though criticizes the use of Roboto font instead of San Francisco. The keyboard launched as iOS-only, with Android users ironically clamoring for it, and includes clever emoji features like predictive emoji suggestions.
key_points: Gboard's privacy policy is surprisingly good: only search queries go to Google, all other typing stays on-device, and there's no way to sign into a Google account even if you want to
Google made the unusual design choice of mimicking iOS's native keyboard rather than using Material Design, but undermined the effort by using Roboto instead of San Francisco
The keyboard launched on iOS first, not Android — a reversal that frustrated Android users
Gboard includes clever emoji features like predictive emoji suggestions and built-in emoji search, addressing a gap in iOS's standard keyboard
Google's lead engineer confirmed the privacy-first approach was their own decision, not an Apple-imposed constraint
money_quotes: My first thought, of course, was 'Sounds like a privacy disaster — Google will see and log everything people type with this keyboard.'
This privacy policy could change in the future, of course. Deals can be altered, and Google's history of deliberately circumventing iOS privacy features is well-documented.
Whether this is Google's own magnanimous decision, a technical limitation in iOS, or a policy decision enforced by App Store review, I don't know.

I think the whole Material Design thing feels terribly out of place on iOS. I'm glad they didn't do it with Gboard, but they should have gone the whole way and used San Francisco for the typeface, too.
I'll bet many users will think Gboard is only adding a search bar above the system keyboard.
Also, they chose too thin a weight of Roboto — I can barely see the period on their '.' key.
key_insight: Google made a surprisingly privacy-respecting and design-conscious iOS keyboard, but undercut its own effort to blend in by using the wrong typeface.
